poster_title: Revisiting Hallucination Detection with Effective Rank-based Uncertainty
authors: Rui Wang, Zeming Wei, Guanzhang Yue, Meng Sun (Peking University)

content: LLMs have transformed AI with generative and reasoning capabilities.
Hallucinations pose a significant challenge for trustworthy deployment.
High-stakes domains like healthcare and scientific discovery are particularly affected.
Existing UQ methods are computationally impractical or add system complexity.
A new, efficient method is needed to detect hallucinations in LLMs.
Hallucinations are factually incorrect but contextually plausible outputs.
They are particularly dangerous in high-stakes domains.
Current UQ methods are not suitable for real-time applications.
A lightweight, training-free method is required to enhance trustworthiness.
Quantify uncertainty using effective rank of hidden states.
Measure semantic variations through spectral analysis.
Detect semantic divergences indicative of hallucinations.
Conducted extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets.
Evaluated performance in detecting hallucinations.
Assessed efficiency and scalability of the method.
panels.figure_caption: Examples of detecting hallucinations using Effective Rank-based Uncertainty

content: Effective rank is a highly predictive signal for hallucination detection.
Consistently outperforms or matches established strong baselines.
Significantly efficient and scalable for real-time applications.
Spectral analysis provides interpretable insights into internal reasoning.
Effective rank detects semantic divergences without additional tools.
Method is lightweight and training-free, suitable for practical use.
Proposed method offers a new paradigm for hallucination detection.
Enhances trustworthiness and reliability of LLMs.
Further research needed on internal-external semantic relationships.

Fig1.caption: Examples of detecting hallucinations using Effective Rank-based Uncertainty
